title: Collecting a landed swarm
subtitle: Hiving bees that have clustered on a branch or post.
excerpt: A swarm that has landed and formed a cluster is usually calm and manageable. It won't stay long, scout bees are already out looking for a permanent home. Here's how to collect it cleanly into a box and hive it before it moves on.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), swarm-management section. BBKA swarm collection guidance. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, swarm chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cluster of bees that has left the original colony, typically containing the old queen and around half the worker bees.
A worker bee that flies ahead of a swarm to locate and assess potential nesting sites.

content.text: A
swarm

that has settled into a cluster on a low branch or fence post is in the most approachable state it will ever be. The bees are gorged on honey from the original hive, their colony instinct is suspended, and there is no brood to defend. This doesn't mean they can't sting, it means they won't unless provoked. Work quietly, use no sudden movements, and the whole operation should go smoothly.
Before you go out

Take a brood box with at least five drawn frames or frames of wax foundation, a crown board, a roof, and a floor. A nuc box works for a small swarm. Suit up fully before you approach. Have a smoker lit but keep it back, smoke agitates swarms more than it calms them. You rarely need it.
Assessing the cluster

Before you touch anything, spend a minute watching. A cluster the size of a rugby ball is a good-sized primary swarm with the old queen. A cluster the size of a grapefruit is a cast, a secondary swarm led by a virgin queen. Both are collected the same way; a cast will take longer to build up.
Check what the cluster is resting on. A flexible branch you can cut or bend is easy. A solid fence post or a brick wall is harder, you won't be able to shake the cluster in one movement.
Collecting from a branch
Place the brood box on the ground directly below the cluster, floor board up, crown board off.
Hold the branch with one hand. Give it one sharp, decisive shake over the open box. Most of the cluster will drop straight in.

Watch the bees on the ground and on surrounding surfaces. If the cluster of bees on the ground begins to march toward the box rather than moving randomly, the queen is in the box. If they begin to regroup elsewhere, the queen missed the shake.
Once the majority are in the box, lay the frames gently on top of them, drop the crown board on, and fit the roof with the entrance open.
Collecting from a fixed surface

If the cluster is on a post or wall, place the brood box entrance directly below it. Use a soft brush or your gloved hand to gently scoop the cluster into the box in sections. Work from the bottom of the cluster upward. As with the branch method, watch whether the walking bees march into the box or reassemble outside it.
Moving the box to its permanent site
Leave the box at the collection site until dusk.
Scout bees

will still be out when you collect the cluster; they need to return to the box before nightfall or they're lost. Once the flight has stopped, close the entrance with a foam bung and move the box to its permanent site. Open the entrance first thing in the morning.

attrs.title: What to do with a swarm you didn't collect in time

content.text: A swarm that moves on before you can collect it is not your problem. It will find a cavity somewhere and set up independently. If it goes into a chimney or cavity wall on a neighbour's property, that becomes their issue to manage; BBKA local associations often maintain a swarm-collector list and will send someone out.
After hiving

Check the hived swarm after three days. If the queen is laying, you'll see tiny eggs at the base of cells in the drawn comb. A swarm with a queen that's already mated (primary swarm) will start laying immediately. A cast with a virgin queen won't lay for another week or two, she needs to mate first.
